title: The Moralizer Corrected. A Tale.
content:
آ آ آ آ A hermit (or if ï؟½chance you hold
آ آ آ آ That title now too trite and old),
آ آ آ آ A man, once young, who lived retired
آ آ آ آ As hermit could have well desired,
آ آ آ آ His hours of study closed at last,
آ آ آ آ And finishï؟½d his concise repast,
آ آ آ آ Stoppled his cruise, replaced his book
آ آ آ آ Within its customary nook,
آ آ آ آ And, staff in hand, set forth to share
آ آ آ آ The sober cordial of sweet air,
آ آ آ آ Like Isaac, with a mind applied
آ آ آ آ To serious thought at evening-tide.
آ آ آ آ Autumnal rains had made it chill,
آ آ آ آ And from the trees, that fringed his hill,
آ آ آ آ Shades slanting at the close of day,
آ آ آ آ Chillï؟½d more his else delightful way.
آ آ آ آ Distant a little mile he spied
آ آ آ آ A western bankï؟½s still sunny side,
آ آ آ آ And right toward the favourï؟½d place
آ آ آ آ Proceeding with his nimblest pace,
آ آ آ آ In hope to bask a little yet,
آ آ آ آ Just reachï؟½d it when the sun was set.
آ آ آ آ Your hermit, young and jovial sirs!
آ آ آ آ Learns something from whateï؟½er occursï؟½
آ آ آ آ And hence, he said, my mind computes
آ آ آ آ The real worth of manï؟½s pursuits.
آ آ آ آ His object chosen, wealth or fame,
آ آ آ آ Or other sublunary game,
آ آ آ آ Imagination to his view
آ آ آ آ Presents it deckï؟½d with every hue,
آ آ آ آ That can seduce him not to spare
آ آ آ آ His powers of best exertion there,
آ آ آ آ But youth, health, vigour to expend
آ آ آ آ On so desirable an end.
آ آ آ آ Ere long approach lifeï؟½s evening shades,
آ آ آ آ The glow that fancy gave it fades;
آ آ آ آ And, earnï؟½d too late, it wants the grace
آ آ آ آ That first engaged him in the chase.
آ آ آ آ True, answerï؟½d an angelic guide,
آ آ آ آ Attendant at the seniorï؟½s sideï؟½
آ آ آ آ But whether all the time it cost
آ آ آ آ To urge the fruitless chase be lost,
آ آ آ آ Must be decided by the worth
آ آ آ آ Of that which callï؟½d his ardour forth.
آ آ آ آ Trifles pursued, whateï؟½er the event,
آ آ آ آ Must cause him shame or discontent;
آ آ آ آ A vicious object still is worse,
آ آ آ آ Successful there, he wins a curse;
آ آ آ آ But he, whom eï؟½en in lifeï؟½s last stage
آ آ آ آ Endeavours laudable engage,
آ آ آ آ Is paid at least in peace of mind,
آ آ آ آ And sense of having well designï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ And if, ere he attain his end,
آ آ آ آ His sun precipitate descend,
آ آ آ آ A brighter prize than that he meant
آ آ آ آ Shall recompense his mere intent.
آ آ آ آ No virtuous wish can bear a date
آ آ آ آ Either too early or too late.
meaning:
William Cowper's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "The Moralizer Corrected. A Tale."...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
